sections.title: Incumbent Technology Profiles (used for satisfaction recalibration)
sections.content:
| Technology | Market Share | Mechanism | Strengths | Weaknesses |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Wired panel-integrated alarm | ~50% | Alarm zone wired to central panel; panel feeds SIEM | IP-native integration, PACS audit trail, low per-event latency, no battery maintenance | Mains-dependent (UPS required for power events), high retrofit cost, zone-failure risk, requires explicit door enrollment |
| Wireless IoT door alarm guard (Türwächter category) | ~30% | Battery-powered radio alarm guard; proprietary 868 MHz protocol to IP gateway | No conduit, battery fail-safe, mechanical alarm independent of power | EasyWave→IP gateway translation breaks audit chain, RF attenuation in metal-dense halls, surface-mount only |
| Mechanical-only alarm | ~20% | Handle-mounted piezo alarm, no radio | Zero installation complexity, zero power dependency | Zero remote monitoring, zero digital audit trail — cannot satisfy SOC 2 CC6.4 |

---

sections.title: Satisfaction Recalibration
sections.content:
One score recalibrated from Step 06 initial estimate based on incumbent evidence:

| Need | Original Sat | Recalibrated Sat | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| E1 — Monitoring perimeter completeness at deployment | 5 | **4** | Wired panel (50% share) requires explicit door enrollment in zone programming. In retrofit legacy DCs — the dominant deployment scenario — secondary exits not pre-wired are routinely absent from panel zone maps. Incumbent weakness on retrofit completeness lowers market satisfaction below initial estimate. |

All other scores confirmed by incumbent evidence (FIT analysis key confirmations: E8 sat=6 ✓ via UPS partial coverage; E18 sat=3 ✓ via EasyWave→IP chain-of-custody gap; E7 sat=3 ✓ via no heartbeat-vs-alarm-test distinction; PRN-1 sat=2 ✓ via no cryptographic audit chain in wireless category).

---

| PRN-2 | RF channel contention from concurrent 868 MHz broadcasts across 50+ door deployment causing delayed/dropped alarm events during high-activity period | product_constraint | 7 | 3 | **11** | — | — | ✓ |
| PRN-3 | Battery replacement across 50+ door DC deployment without triggering accidental alarms or passing any door through unmonitored state | product_constraint | 6 | 4 | **8** | — | — | ✓ |
| PRN-4 | Firmware version and radio protocol configuration verification across all deployed units via central BUS gateway without physical door access | product_constraint | 7 | 2 | **12** | — | — | ✓ |

### Top-5 Opportunities (by opportunity score)

sections.title: Cross-Check vs. Feature-Market Fit (Step 09)
sections.content:
| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| ODI avg opportunity score | 11.91 |
| Feature-Fit avg (Step 09) | 4.50 (across 6 technology features) |

**Threshold check:**
- ODI avg > 12 AND Feature-Fit avg < 4 → **No flag** (ODI = 11.91, FMF = 4.50)
- ODI avg < 8 AND Feature-Fit avg > 7 → **No flag**

**Interpretation:** No inconsistency threshold triggered. However, the pattern is meaningful: moderate technology fit (4.50) against high unmet need (10 of 23 entries underserved). The Türwächter addresses the market's physical safety requirements (Battery-Powered Operation = 6.44, highest feature fit) but the compliance audit chain — the market's #1 and #2 opportunities — sits below the product-level scope and requires the EasyWave→IP gateway layer, which is where fit degrades (Wireless BUS Interface = 4 vs. incumbent 8). This is not an inconsistency; it is a coherent signal: the product fits the fire-safety job but is under-equipped for the audit-evidence job.

---

rows.Mechanism: Alarm zone wired to central panel; panel feeds SIEM
rows.Strengths: IP-native integration, PACS audit trail, low per-event latency, no battery maintenance
rows.Weaknesses: Mains-dependent (UPS required for power events), high retrofit cost, zone-failure risk, requires explicit door enrollment

rows.Technology: Wireless IoT door alarm guard (Türwächter category)
rows.Mechanism: Battery-powered radio alarm guard; proprietary 868 MHz protocol to IP gateway
rows.Strengths: No conduit, battery fail-safe, mechanical alarm independent of power
rows.Weaknesses: EasyWave→IP gateway translation breaks audit chain, RF attenuation in metal-dense halls, surface-mount only

rows.Strengths: Zero installation complexity, zero power dependency
rows.Weaknesses: Zero remote monitoring, zero digital audit trail — cannot satisfy SOC 2 CC6.4

rows.Need: E1 — Monitoring perimeter completeness at deployment
rows.Reason: Wired panel (50% share) requires explicit door enrollment in zone programming. In retrofit legacy DCs — the dominant deployment scenario — secondary exits not pre-wired are routinely absent from panel zone maps. Incumbent weakness on retrofit completeness lowers market satisfaction below initial estimate.

entries.error_statement: Minimize the likelihood of emergency exit doors being excluded from the SOC 2 CC6.4 monitoring perimeter due to incomplete door inventory at deployment, resulting in >0 unmonitored exits in audit scope
entries.recalibration_note: Lowered from sat=5: wired panel (50% share) requires explicit door enrollment; legacy DC secondary exits routinely absent from zone maps

entries.error_statement: Minimize the likelihood of alert threshold misconfiguration at deployment resulting in >5% false positive door alarm events per week, degrading Security Operations Center alert response discipline
Increase the ability to complete RF coverage planning and gateway placement for a 50-door deployment using a standard site survey toolkit in ≤1 day of engineer effort
Minimize the likelihood of receiver placement errors causing >5% of installed door guardian units to report intermittent connectivity loss in a server hall with dense metal rack infrastructure
Increase the ability to install and commission a door guardian unit on any standard commercial exit door within ≤15 minutes without specialist low-voltage contractor skills
Minimize the likelihood of incorrect pairing between door guardian radio transmitter and IP gateway causing alarm events to be silently dropped during initial deployment verification
Increase the ability to verify end-to-end alarm event propagation from each door guardian to the SIEM log within ≤30 seconds of a manual test activation across all 50+ installed units simultaneously
Minimize the likelihood of door guardian units appearing as online in the gateway dashboard but failing to transmit alarm events under Faraday cage RF conditions, creating undetected monitoring gaps

Minimize the likelihood of an emergency exit door activation going unlogged in the SIEM audit trail during a facility power event — including UPS switchover, generator start sequence, and mains failure
Increase the ability to deliver door activation alerts to the Security Operations Center console within ≤30 seconds of physical door trigger under standard server hall RF conditions
Minimize the likelihood of an emergency exit door activation — including authorized key override bypass — going unlogged in the SIEM event record, permitting 0 unlogged activations per audit period
Minimize the variability in SIEM timestamp accuracy between physical door guardian activation and the corresponding log entry to ≤5 seconds across all deployed units
Increase the ability to predict door guardian battery replacement needs with ≥14 days advance warning per unit, enabling scheduled maintenance without emergency callouts
Minimize the likelihood of a door guardian unit going offline without automated SOC notification, creating a silent monitoring gap exceeding 15 minutes
Minimize the variability in wireless signal quality for door guardian units installed in server halls with variable rack density and AI/GPU thermal load shifts that alter electromagnetic propagation
Increase the ability to update door guardian monitoring configuration — add/remove doors, change alert thresholds — without on-site field work per door, completing a 10-door change in ≤1 hour
Minimize the time to restore full door monitoring coverage to 100% of in-scope exits following a gateway or receiver failure, maintaining SOC 2 audit continuity within ≤4 hours mean recovery time
